original_abstract:
To address the mounting energy and speed bottlenecks in modern computer vision, we present ACCEL—an all-analog photoelectronic chip that synergizes diffractive optical analog computing (OAC) with electronic analog computing (EAC). ACCEL operates by first utilizing a multilayer optical encoder to extract high-dimensional features at the speed of light. These optical signals are converted into photocurrents that directly drive an integrated analog electronic circuit, eliminating the need for power-hungry analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) and significantly reducing latency.

Experimentally, ACCEL achieves a systemic computing speed of 4.6 peta-operations per second (Peta-OPS) and an energy efficiency of 74.8 Peta-OPS per watt—performance metrics that exceed state-of-the-art digital processors by one and three orders of magnitude, respectively. We demonstrate competitive classification accuracies on MNIST (97.1%), Fashion-MNIST (85.5%), and 3-class ImageNet (82.0%), alongside high-speed time-lapse video recognition (92.6%). Notably, our architecture maintains high robustness in ultra-low-light environments (0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame) and utilizes an adaptive training method to mitigate manufacturing and alignment errors. By fusing the scalability of optics with the flexibility of electronics in a single analog pipeline, ACCEL provides a high-performance, low-power solution for real-time vision applications such as autonomous driving, robotics, and industrial inspection.
humanized_abstract:
To address the energy and speed bottlenecks in computer vision, we developed an all-analog photoelectronic chip named ACCEL (ACCEL) that leverages diffractive optical analog computing (OAC) and electronic analog computing (EAC). The ACCEL chip features a multilayer optical encoder to achieve fast feature extraction in high dimensionality at the speed of light, and it utilizes optical signals generated by the features to instantly produce photocurrents that drive an integrated analog electronic circuit without requiring the use of expensive ADCs.

All Optical Co-processor Link at the Edge of Computing (ACCEL) achieves a system processing throughput of 4.6 Peta-OPS and energy efficiency of 74.8 Peta-OPS per watt, an order of magnitude and three orders of magnitude faster and more energy efficient, respectively, than existing digital electronics. While matching the classification accuracy of state-of-the-art deep learning techniques with MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and 3-class ImageNet datasets, ACCEL’s unconventional optical implementation for vision processing achieves the fastest time-lapse video classification at 92.6% for object location at 8 frames per second. The implementation also achieves robust performance in ultra-low-light level (LL), reaching the lowest measured value of 0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame over an optical image extent of 0.45 mm². An adaptive training approach built into ACCEL’s training and fabrication process also helps mitigate the effects of processing variations and errors associated with optical system assembly and component misalignment. Overall, by integrating the advantages of optics and electronics into one analog “look-through” processing path, ACCEL could potentially revolutionize the field of vision processing through higher compute rate, better energy efficiency and real-time operation in applications ranging from self-driving vehicles to robots and cameras in industrial production lines.

document.output:
To address the energy and speed bottlenecks in computer vision, we developed an all-analog photoelectronic chip named ACCEL (ACCEL) that leverages diffractive optical analog computing (OAC) and electronic analog computing (EAC). The ACCEL chip features a multilayer optical encoder to achieve fast feature extraction in high dimensionality at the speed of light, and it utilizes optical signals generated by the features to instantly produce photocurrents that drive an integrated analog electronic circuit without requiring the use of expensive ADCs.

All Optical Co-processor Link at the Edge of Computing (ACCEL) achieves a system processing throughput of 4.6 Peta-OPS and energy efficiency of 74.8 Peta-OPS per watt, an order of magnitude and three orders of magnitude faster and more energy efficient, respectively, than existing digital electronics. While matching the classification accuracy of state-of-the-art deep learning techniques with MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and 3-class ImageNet datasets, ACCEL’s unconventional optical implementation for vision processing achieves the fastest time-lapse video classification at 92.6% for object location at 8 frames per second. The implementation also achieves robust performance in ultra-low-light level (LL), reaching the lowest measured value of 0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame over an optical image extent of 0.45 mm². Overall, by integrating the advantages of optics and electronics into one analog “look-through” processing path, ACCEL could potentially revolutionize the field of vision processing through higher compute rate, better energy efficiency and real-time operation in applications ranging from self-driving vehicles to robots and cameras in industrial production lines.
document.input:
To address the mounting energy and speed bottlenecks in modern computer vision, we present ACCEL—an all-analog photoelectronic chip that synergizes diffractive optical analog computing (OAC) with electronic analog computing (EAC). ACCEL operates by first utilizing a multilayer optical encoder to extract high-dimensional features at the speed of light. These optical signals are converted into photocurrents that directly drive an integrated analog electronic circuit, eliminating the need for power-hungry analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) and significantly reducing latency.

Experimentally, ACCEL achieves a systemic computing speed of 4.6 peta-operations per second (Peta-OPS) and an energy efficiency of 74.8 Peta-OPS per watt—performance metrics that exceed state-of-the-art digital processors by one and three orders of magnitude, respectively. We demonstrate competitive classification accuracies on MNIST (97.1%), Fashion-MNIST (85.5%), and 3-class ImageNet (82.0%), alongside high-speed time-lapse video recognition (92.6%). Notably, our architecture maintains high robustness in ultra-low-light environments (0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame) and utilizes an adaptive training method to mitigate manufacturing and alignment errors. By fusing the scalability of optics with the flexibility of electronics in a single analog pipeline, ACCEL provides a high-performance, low-power solution for real-time vision applications such as autonomous driving, robotics, and industrial inspection.
